structured_choices.data_source: Historical sensor readings and maintenance logs from industrial machinery spanning 5 years (60 months). Data collected at 1-minute intervals from thousands of machines. Source: production environment sensors and maintenance event logs. Time period: months 1–60 of available data.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary classification: equipment failure occurrence within a 7-day forward window from the end of each observation window. A failure is defined as a maintenance log entry marked as 'unplanned downtime' with root cause traced to equipment malfunction (not external supply chain or operational procedure changes). Labeling performed retrospectively on maintenance logs with manual review of 5% of borderline cases for consistency.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with machine-level stratification. Machines are randomly assigned to train (60%), validation (20%), or test (20%) cohorts, held constant across all time periods. For each cohort, data is split chronologically: months 1–36 for training, months 37–48 for validation, months 49–60 for test. This temporal approach prevents future information leakage and reflects real deployment conditions (model trained on past, evaluated on unseen future). Machine-level stratification ensures each cohort has similar machinery distributions and failure rates, controlling for equipment heterogeneity.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the training set only (months 1–36, train machines). Fitted transformers (scaling parameters, imputation statistics, sensor normalization constants) are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Time series windowing logic (e.g., constructing 7-day rolling windows for each observation point) is applied consistently across all splits after the temporal boundary is established, ensuring no temporal leakage.
structured_choices.features: Raw sensor streams (e.g., temperature, vibration, pressure, electrical current) are windowed into fixed-length sequences: 1,008 observations per window (7 days × 1,440 minutes/day), sampled at 1-minute resolution. Univariate time series features per sensor: (1) raw normalized values, (2) first-order differences (delta), (3) rolling 1-hour mean and standard deviation, (4) rolling 24-hour mean and standard deviation. Engineered metadata: machine age (months in service), machine type (categorical, one-hot encoded), prior failure count (in preceding 12 months). No features engineering the label or incorporating maintenance log information outside the observation window. Missing sensor values are imputed using forward-fill within a 60-minute window; gaps >60 minutes result in window exclusion.
structured_choices.model: Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM-based) with the following architecture: input layer for multivariate sensor sequences, two bidirectional LSTM layers (128 and 64 units), dropout (0.2) after each LSTM layer, fully connected layers (64 and 32 units, ReLU activation), and sigmoid output for binary classification. The model operates on fixed 1,008-length sequences and produces a single prediction per window (failure within 7 days: yes/no). Model is trained using Adam optimizer with binary cross-entropy loss, early stopping on validation loss (patience=10 epochs), and batch size 32.
structured_choices.baseline: Statistical Process Control (SPC) baseline using traditional control charts (X-bar and moving range charts) applied to each sensor independently. For each machine and sensor, control limits are established from training data (months 1–36): center line = training mean, upper/lower limits = mean ± 2.66 × moving range / 1.128 (standard SPC thresholds). A failure alert is triggered if any sensor exceeds its control limits for ≥3 consecutive measurements. This baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: control limit parameters are optimized on the validation set (months 37–48) by grid search over threshold multipliers (1.5, 2.0, 2.66, 3.0) and consecutive-violation counts (2, 3, 4, 5) to maximize validation-set F1 score. The baseline uses the same raw sensor data and metadata as the RNN, ensuring a fair comparison.
structured_choices.primary_metric: F1 score (harmonic mean of precision and recall). Justification: The manufacturing stakeholder's objective is to reduce unplanned downtime, which depends on both detecting failures (recall) and avoiding false alarms that trigger unnecessary maintenance (precision). F1 balances these trade-offs and is robust to class imbalance. F1 is computed at the default decision threshold (0.5 predicted probability). It directly translates to operational impact: higher F1 means more failures caught before they occur (reducing downtime) without excessive false positives (which waste maintenance resources and can erode trust in the system).
secondary_metrics: Precision: critical for controlling false-alarm costs and maintenance resource allocation.
Recall (sensitivity): critical for understanding how many failures are caught; directly related to downtime reduction.
ROC-AUC: model discrimination ability across decision thresholds; used to assess if the RNN learns a meaningful signal.
Positive predictive value (PPV) and negative predictive value (NPV): operational metrics showing reliability of predictions in real deployment.

Downtime hours averted (simulated): post-hoc calculation assuming each true positive prevents 8 hours of downtime, each false positive costs 2 hours in maintenance scheduling, each false negative costs 48 hours in unplanned downtime. Provides direct translation to business KPI but is only used for interpretation, not model selection.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 37–48, machines in validation cohort) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. The RNN is trained on the training set, and performance is evaluated on the validation set at each epoch. Hyperparameter grid: learning rate (1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3), LSTM units (64, 128, 256), dropout (0.1, 0.2, 0.3), batch size (16, 32, 64). The model configuration yielding the highest validation F1 score is selected. The test set is not examined or used for any tuning decisions.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 49–60, machines in test cohort) is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete. Final performance metrics (F1, precision, recall, ROC-AUC, PPV, NPV) are computed on the test set using the selected model without any modification or retraining. These test results are reported as the primary evidence for or against the hypothesis. No threshold tuning or post-hoc adjustments are made based on test performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: changes in operational practices or external disruptions (supply chain, staffing) could reduce downtime independently of the model. Mitigation: (1) Stratify the test set by machine type and age to ensure the RNN and baseline are evaluated on equivalent machinery distributions. (2) Perform stratified analysis of test performance by time period (months 49–54 vs. 55–60) to detect if performance degrades during known disruption windows; report results separately. (3) Exclude maintenance events with root cause = 'supply chain delay' or 'procedure change' from the label definition; use only equipment-malfunction-rooted failures. (4) Document any major operational changes during months 37–60 and perform sensitivity analysis: remove 4-week windows post-disruption and re-report test metrics. (5) Baseline vs. RNN comparison isolates the prediction model's contribution: both use identical raw data and are evaluated on the same ground truth, so differences in performance attributable to the modeling approach, not data or external factors. The experiment establishes whether an RNN outperforms SPC for this task; it does not directly establish whether the maintenance intervention itself reduces downtime—that requires a randomized deployment trial (recommended as follow-up).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN-Based Predictive Maintenance vs. Statistical Process Control

### 1. Data Strategy

We will use 5 years (60 months) of continuous sensor readings and maintenance logs from the production environment. Sensors record temperature, vibration, pressure, and electrical current at 1-minute intervals across thousands of machines. The label is defined retrospectively from maintenance logs: a positive label indicates an unplanned downtime event occurred within a 7-day forward window, where the root cause is documented as equipment malfunction (not external disruptions like supply chain delays or operational procedure changes). Approximately 5% of borderline or unclear maintenance records will be manually reviewed by domain experts to ensure consistent labeling. Known data quality issues include sensor noise, occasional missing readings (addressed via forward-fill within 60 minutes; windows with longer gaps are excluded), and sensor drift (handled by per-sensor normalization during preprocessing).

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a temporal split with machine-level stratification. Machines are randomly partitioned into train (60%), validation (20%), and test (20%) cohorts at the outset, with this assignment held constant across all months. Within each cohort, data is divided chronologically: months 1–36 for training, months 37–48 for validation, and months 49–60 for testing. This temporal approach is essential for this hypothesis because it reflects real deployment conditions (the model will never see future data in production) and eliminates information leakage that would occur with random temporal shuffling. Machine-level stratification ensures that each cohort has similar machinery types, ages, and intrinsic failure rates, preventing confounding by equipment heterogeneity. This split method is appropriate given the time series structure and the manufacturing context, where deployed models must generalize to held-out future time periods.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing and feature transformers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). For each sensor, we construct fixed-length windows of 1,008 observations (7 days at 1-minute resolution). Raw values are normalized using z-score scaling (fit on training data mean and std). We engineer four sensor-level features: (1) normalized raw values, (2) first-order differences (rates of change), (3) 1-hour rolling mean and std, and (4) 24-hour rolling mean and std. Machine metadata includes age in service (in months), machine type (one-hot encoded), and failure count in the preceding 12 months. Missing sensor values within a 60-minute window are forward-filled; longer gaps result in window exclusion. Critically, no features incorporate the outcome label or information from outside the observation window, preventing label leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RNN Model:** A bidirectional LSTM network with two stacked LSTM layers (128 and 64 units), dropout (0.2 after each layer), followed by fully connected layers (64 and 32 units, ReLU), and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. The model processes fixed-length multivariate sensor sequences and outputs a single prediction per window (failure within 7 days: yes/no).

**Baseline:** Statistical Process Control using traditional X-bar and moving-range control charts applied independently to each sensor per machine. For each sensor, control limits are derived from training data: center line = mean, upper/lower control limits = mean ± 2.66 × (mean moving range / 1.128). A failure alert is triggered if any sensor exceeds its limits for ≥3 consecutive measurements. The baseline is tuned on the validation set via grid search over control limit multipliers (1.5 to 3.0) and consecutive-violation thresholds (2 to 5), optimizing for F1 score. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and use identical raw sensor data, ensuring a fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** F1 score. This metric is chosen because the manufacturing stakeholder's objective—reducing unplanned downtime—depends on both detecting failures (recall) and minimizing false alarms (precision). F1 balances these trade-offs and translates directly to operational impact: high F1 means more failures are caught before they occur without excessive false positives that waste maintenance resources.

**Secondary Metrics:** Precision, recall, ROC-AUC, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value provide granular insight into model performance. A post-hoc simulation calculates estimated downtime hours averted (true positives × 8 hours prevented, minus false positives × 2 hours wasted, minus false negatives × 48 hours unplanned) for business context, though this does not drive model selection.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 37–48). The RNN explores a grid of learning rates (1e-4 to 1e-3), LSTM units (64 to 256), dropout rates (0.1 to 0.3), and batch sizes (16 to 64). The configuration maximizing validation F1 is selected. Early stopping is applied during training using validation loss (patience = 10 epochs). The test set is held entirely separate and is evaluated exactly once after model selection, without modification or retuning.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

We control for operational confounds by: (1) excluding maintenance events with non-equipment-malfunction root causes from the label; (2) stratifying test results by machine type and age; (3) analyzing test performance by time period (months 49–54 vs. 55–60) to detect performance changes during known disruptions; and (4) performing sensitivity analyses excluding 4-week windows after documented disruptions. Both the RNN and baseline are evaluated on identical ground truth and data, isolating the modeling approach's contribution. The experiment establishes whether an RNN predicts failures better than SPC but does not directly prove that the resulting maintenance intervention reduces downtime—a follow-up randomized deployment trial would be needed for that claim.
